question: Will the two leaders reach a settlement within 60 days?

mediator.description: The neutral party facilitating the mediated settlement process

campaign_resource.description: Resources consumed by the contested campaign (financial, material, personnel)

settlement_document.description: A written settlement framework or proposal

settlement_approval_record.description: Record of a leader's decision on a settlement proposal

campaign_cost_projection.description: Projected costs of continuing the contested campaign

leads_sides_in_mediation.description: Which leader represents which side in mediated talks

controls_resource.description: Which leader controls which campaign resource

settlement_proposal_submitted.description: A formal settlement proposal is submitted for approval

approval_record_created.description: A leader records their approval decision on a settlement document

campaign_cost_increased.description: Costs of the contested campaign rise significantly

unmodeled_actor_action.description: schema-scoped scaffolding: an actor action whose semantics could not be compiled; exact content preserved; counted as a fallback

mediated_settlement_process.description: The multi-step process from framework to final settlement

cost_pressure_process.description: Rising costs create pressure to settle

settlement_approval_board.description: The decision-making body composed of both leaders to approve/reject a settlement

aggregation.description: Both leaders must approve for settlement to be reached

settlement_requires_physical_document.description: Settlement documents must be physically signed or digitally authenticated
settlement_requires_physical_document.rule: Both leaders must submit approval_record with approval_decision='approve' for same document_id

costs_are_rising.description: Both sides face rising costs from the contested campaign

financial_reserve.description: Each leader's financial reserves for continuing the campaign

cost_projections_are_private.description: Each leader has private knowledge of their own campaign cost projections
cost_projections_are_private.rule: campaign_cost_projection records are only visible to the owning leader unless shared in mediation

approval_records_are_public.description: Once a leader submits an approval record, it is visible to all parties
approval_records_are_public.rule: settlement_approval_record becomes visible to both leaders and mediator upon creation

leader_a.why_consequential: Must approve any settlement for it to be reached; controls campaign resources
affordances: Submit settlement approval record with 'approve', 'reject', or 'amend_request'
Continue or escalate the contested campaign

leader_b.why_consequential: Must approve any settlement for it to be reached; controls campaign resources
affordances: Submit settlement approval record with 'approve', 'reject', or 'amend_request'
Continue or escalate the contested campaign

mediator.why_consequential: Controls the mediation process; can submit settlement proposals
affordances: Share cost projections between sides with permission

outcome_predicates.option_true: Both leaders submitted 'approve' for the same document_id before 2024-01-13
outcome_predicates.option_false: At least one leader submitted 'reject' or no approval by deadline

unresolved_mechanisms: Whether the existing 'framework' is substantive enough to produce a final document within the horizon
How rising costs translate into specific pressure on each leader's decision timeline

assumptions.description: Both leaders must approve the same settlement document for settlement to be reached
assumptions.evidence: Implied by 'two-party mediated settlement' and no mention of alternative decision rules

assumptions.description: Costs of the contested campaign continue to increase during the prediction horizon
assumptions.evidence: Evidence states 'both sides face rising costs'

missing_decisive_elements: No representation of deadlines, ultimatums, or time pressure mechanisms (e.g., '60-day clock' or 'drop-dead date') that would drive urgency
No model of exogenous shocks or spoiler events (e.g., military incident, economic crisis, third-party intervention) that could collapse or accelerate the process
No representation of leader's domestic political constraints (e.g., approval ratings, election cycles, coalition pressures) that often determine settlement timing
No capacity to model sequential offers/counteroffers with concession dynamics (the process only has 'proposal_drafted' then 'submitted' with no iteration)
relabeled_progress_bars: The 'mediated_settlement_process' state machine is a linear progress bar (framework -> mediation -> proposal -> approval -> settled/failed) that assumes deterministic forward movement, not realistic back-and-forth
The 'cost_pressure_process' is a monotonic escalation bar (stable -> rising -> critical) that ignores cost reduction, negotiation leverage shifts, or external funding changes
hidden_answer_risks: The model assumes settlement failure only from explicit rejection records, but a leader could simply stall indefinitely (no 'timeout' or 'no decision' state) — this would make the 60-day question unanswerable
The 'both_approvals_received' trigger is never defined as an event type; it's a composite condition with no explicit detection logic, risking ghost transitions
The 60-day question horizon is not wired into any process; there is no time-bound trigger or timeout transition for the mediated_settlement_process
direct_human_reaction_encodings: Emotional states like 'trust breakdown', 'anger', 'face-saving' are entirely absent, yet these often determine whether leaders approve or reject
No representation of private backchannel communications or informal deals that precede formal approval records
The 'campaign_cost_increased' event has no link to leader psychology; rising costs might harden resolve (sunk cost fallacy) rather than create settlement pressure
public_posture_vs_private_reality_confla: The model only captures official 'settlement_approval_record' decisions (approve/reject/amend_request), but leaders often signal approval publicly while privately blocking, or vice versa — no distinction is made
Mediation sessions have an 'outcome_summary' field but no mechanism to separate public spin from actual progress
No way to model leaked information or disinformation that alters perceptions of the other side's intentions
missing_nonhuman_mechanisms: No representation of algorithmic trading or financial market reactions that could force settlement (e.g., currency crash, bond yield spikes)
No model of environmental factors (weather, natural disasters) that might disrupt logistics or create humanitarian imperatives
No cyber operations, electronic warfare, or infrastructure sabotage that could independently escalate or de-escalate the campaign
